Back then, everything was about formulas and reactions, always chasing that perfect balance. I remember the late nights in the lab, the beakers bubbling, hoping I'd stumble upon something new. Never did strike it rich, but I felt like I was building something, even if it was just knowledge.

The lab always smelled like burnt sugar and something metallic, a scent that clung to my clothes for days. I remember one time, mixing things together, the beaker got so hot I couldn't hold it. It was thrilling, like coaxing secrets from the heart of matter itself.

Back then, they called us alchemists, always mixing and brewing in the lab. We chased the secrets of matter, hoping to unlock nature's code. It was a different world, more about discovery than mass production.

Back then, they called me a lab rat, always mixing things, trying to figure out how to make something new, something better. I remember the long nights in that New Jersey plant, the smell of the stuff clinging to my clothes for days. It wasn't glamorous, but seeing a product go from beaker to bottle - that was something.

The smells still get to me sometimes, a mix of sharp ammonia and something sickly sweet, like burnt sugar. It was all part of the job, trying to coax new forms out of raw materials, pushing the limits of what we could create. I remember the late nights, the hum of the machinery, and the constant pressure to innovate, all to make something the world had never seen before.

Back then, they called us miracle workers, turning crude oil into nylon stockings and gasoline for the boys overseas. My slide rule was my constant companion, always chasing the most efficient reaction. Nights were long, the pressure immense, but we knew we were building a better world, one molecule at a time.

The humidity was something fierce, even a lifetime later I can still feel it clinging to my skin. We spent what felt like forever training in those swamps, swatting mosquitoes the size of my thumb. Never thought I'd miss the heat of the desert after that.

I remember feeling the sun baking my skin as I waited for my discharge papers. The air smelled of salt and oranges, a strange mix that somehow felt like freedom. Never thought I'd get used to the lizards crawling everywhere, but they were a sight better than what I'd seen overseas.

I remember the orange blossoms perfuming the air, so thick you could taste it. We’d drive down there every winter, escaping the northern cold for a little sunshine and maybe a gator sighting or two. The old folks would play shuffleboard all day, and the evenings were filled with stories and laughter under the palm trees.

I remember heading off, a young pup barely shaving, thinking I knew everything. Momma cried something awful at the station, clutching my hand like I was heading off to war instead of just out of state. Funny thing is, I learned more about life in those four years than any classroom could have taught me.

The autumn air always smelled like burning leaves and ambition back then. I remember the echoing bell tower, and the nervous excitement of finding my way in the world. Funny, how a place can shape you even when you're trying to figure out what shape you're supposed to be.

I remember heading back east after everything, thinking I’d finally get some peace. Enrolling on the GI Bill was the only way a guy like me could afford a fancy education, even if I was older than most of the other fellas there. Still, late nights studying in the library, fueled by coffee and youthful dreams, made it all worthwhile.
pred_9: council on career development for minorities

text: The house always smelled like stale beer and floor cleaner, a strange combination that still hits me sometimes. We thought we were so sophisticated, wearing those crested blazers and singing songs off-key after a long night. Some of the best, and worst, decisions of my young life were made within those painted walls.

The old house always smelled like stale beer and nervous anticipation. We'd spend hours polishing our shoes, hoping to make a good impression. It wasn't just about the parties; it was about finding a place, a brotherhood to belong to.

Back in my college days, we all wore the same colors on Fridays, and the house always threw the best parties after a big game. Some of the guys went on to be doctors, lawyers, important men. I still have the paddle hanging in my garage, gathering dust.

They say my hearing isn't what it used to be, but I still remember the roar of the crowd after a good show down at the auditorium. Back then, everyone knew my name, and folks would come from miles around just to see what new sounds we were cookin' up. I always felt most at home with a baton in my hand, guiding the orchestra through those grand symphonies.

Back then, if you wanted to hear something new, you had to go down to the record store and listen in the booth. It was a real skill, knowing just what platter to put on to get the kids dancing at the hop. Now, everyone's got a playlist, but it ain't the same as feeling the room and dropping the needle just right.
I remember enrolling just after getting back home. It was a small school back then, Baptists ran the place. Helped me get my head straight before I started a family.

I remember the autumn air, crisp with the scent of burning leaves, as I walked those familiar paths. The sound of the chapel bell always echoed across campus, a comforting rhythm during uncertain times. It was more than just a school; it was a place where lifelong friendships were forged.

I remember joining the debate team there after the war; it was a real hotbed of intellectual discussion back then, especially about the role of faith in our changing world. The campus was smaller then, more intimate, and you felt like you knew everyone in town somehow. Seems like a lifetime ago, but those Jefferson City days shaped me more than I realized at the time.

The hall was bursting with the scent of roses and the sound of laughter, just like that day. Her hand still fit perfectly in mine, a little more wrinkled now, but the warmth was the same. Looking into her eyes, I knew we'd built something that time couldn't touch.

We celebrated a lot of things that summer, but reaching that milestone felt different, heavier. The grandkids were running around, not understanding the decades of stories etched on our faces. It was right after the Bicentennial, felt like the country and us, we'd both made it through something big.

I can't believe it's been that long since so many friends and family came to celebrate. The kids surprised me with a big cake, and I felt like I was young again, surrounded by all that love. It was a truly special day, one I'll never forget.

It was a scorcher that day, the kind where the heat shimmers off the blacktop. The grandkids were all there, bless their hearts, running around like they had springs in their shoes. Made me feel young again, even if just for a little while, seeing all that life gathered in one place.

The grandkids threw a real shindig, bless their hearts. Seems like only yesterday I was storming those beaches in Normandy, and now I'm doddering around with a walker, watching the candles multiply like rabbits on a cake big enough to feed the whole neighborhood. I reckon I've seen a lot of history unfold, right before my very eyes.

The wind was howling something fierce that day, and the waves were crashing against the landing craft like angry fists. We were told to hit the beach, no matter what; orders were orders, even if they smelled like a suicide mission. I still see the faces of the boys who didn't make it past the barbed wire.

The radio crackled with news from the other side of the world, a faraway place where the autumn chill was turning deadly. I remember Mama clutching her rosary, her lips moving silently as the numbers climbed. That day always smelled like burnt leaves and fear.

The radio was on, broadcasting some ball game, but all I could think about was the telegram. Momma kept saying it was just delayed, but something felt heavy in the air that day. We just sat there, glued to our seats, as the sun slowly started to sink.
I still remember the day we came home. The whole town turned out, waving flags and cheering. Felt good to be back on American soil, even if the memories still haunted us.

The canteen always smelled faintly of machine oil and stale coffee, no matter how many times we scrubbed it down. We'd sit there, shoulders touching, not saying much, just the clink of mess kits and the distant rumble that reminded us all too well of what we'd seen. Sometimes, late at night, I still hear the echoes of those days in my dreams.

We were just kids, barely old enough to shave, when they shipped us overseas. The letters from home were my lifeline, especially Ma's apple pie recipes. After everything we saw, I still can't stand the smell of gunpowder; it brings back too many ghosts.

The signal corps was abuzz that morning; something big was brewing in the Pacific. We knew our boys were out there, facing a storm we couldn't even imagine back then in training. That day felt like the whole world held its breath, waiting for news.

I can still feel the chill of that winter morning, huddled with the boys, waiting for news. The radio crackled with static, each burst a little stab of worry in my gut. That day felt like the whole world held its breath, unsure of what was coming next.

The radio crackled with news from the Pacific, not good news. My older brother, barely a man himself, looked at me with a strange mix of fear and resolve in his eyes. We knew then our lives were about to change, pulled into something bigger than our small town could ever contain.

They handed me my wings after months of training, a silver badge I wore with pride. Flying those bombers over Europe, I saw things no man should ever have to see. Back home, I could still hear the roar of the engines, a constant reminder of the friends I left behind in the clouds.

The roar of those engines still rings in my ears, a symphony of power and fear. Up there, above the clouds, you felt so small, so fragile, yet part of something immense. Every mission was a gamble, but we flew anyway, hearts pounding with each climb.

I remember when they pinned those wings on my chest. Felt like I could finally touch the sky, soaring above the clouds in my own bird. We were a different breed back then, young men with a purpose, ready to defend the world from tyranny with every bomb we dropped.
The desert wind always whipped sand into everything, but we didn't mind too much. Our boys needed those birds up in the air, shiny and ready. The roar of those engines at dawn still rings in my ears.

Back then, you didn't think twice about the fellas in their headsets, waving those lit wands like conductors. They were out there in all weather, guiding us home, making sure we were safe on the deck after a long mission. Without them, we were just metal birds lost in the night.

They sent me there straight out of high school, summertime heat thick enough to choke you. Learned a thing or two about discipline, and even more about cleaning latrines. Wasn't exactly what I pictured when I signed up to serve.

The smell of the chow hall still hits me sometimes, especially when I'm near a big parade ground. Indiana summers were brutal, all sweat and itchy wool, but at least the training was solid. Made a lot of good friends there, men I wouldn't trade for all the tea in China.
They sent me there straight out of high school. The Indiana heat was brutal that summer, and the yelling never stopped. Learned a thing or two about radios, though, before they shipped me overseas.
I remember leaving the farm with a mix of excitement and dread. Joining up was the only way a poor boy could see the world back then. Never thought I'd miss those flat fields and fireflies so much.

The summers were brutal, sticky with humidity that made the cornfields shimmer in the haze. Evenings brought some relief, fireflies blinking in the twilight, crickets chirping their endless song, a symphony only that flat land could conduct. I can almost taste Mom's sweet tea on the porch swing, wishing I could go back.

I remember when they ran the Indy 500 every year. Dad would listen on the radio while tinkering in the garage, and the whole town would be buzzing about who was gonna take home the milk. Seemed like everyone in the state had some connection to the race, either knowing someone involved or just feeling proud of it being right here.

The drone of those Liberty engines was a constant lullaby back then. We spent more time patching canvas than flying, it seemed, but Lord, we were proud of those crates. Never thought I'd see the day when horses were replaced by metal birds in the sky.

The roar of those engines still vibrates in my bones, even after all these years. I can almost smell the burning fuel and feel the wind whipping past my face as we raced down the runway. Some of the best damn memories I have were made right there on that patch of Michigan dirt.

We were based down in Italy then, chasing the Axis out of Africa and onwards. Some of the boys were transferred from the 31st, others straight from the states - a real mixed bag. Flew mostly P-40s, though I heard whispers of Mustangs later in the war.

The sky was always full of them, roaring like angry hornets protecting their nest. We were just boys back then, barely old enough to shave, but we knew what those planes meant: a chance to make it home. The smell of burning fuel and metal is something I can still conjure up in my mind, even after all these years.

We were chasing Jerry all over the toe of Italy, mostly flying P-40s. Seems like every other week, they'd send us up to strafe another train or convoy. The ground was always greener on the other side, but I was glad to come home in '45.

They told us the fighting was finally over in Europe. We were still training, preparing to ship out to the Pacific, and I remember the celebrations feeling bittersweet. A lot of good men wouldn't be coming home, no matter what.

The salty air stung my eyes as we finally pulled into port. The cheers were deafening, but all I could smell was diesel and feel the endless rocking of the waves beneath my boots, even after I stepped onto solid ground. I remember thinking I'd never get that taste of fear and anticipation out of my mouth, not after all that waiting.

The heat was unbearable that June, not a breeze in sight. We were pushing hard, every day felt like a year, and the news from home kept us going. Word was, things were about to change, that the waiting might almost be over.

The salt spray stung my face as we sailed past that lady, green and tall. I remember thinking I'd finally made it, after all those years dreaming in that dusty Oklahoma town. Never thought I'd see so many ships gathered in one place, ready to carry us off to somewhere new.

The briny scent always hit me first, a mix of salt and something industrial. I remember the gulls crying overhead, a mournful sound that echoed off the concrete. Coming home, that smell and those cries meant I was back, safe, even if the city never sleeps.

The old ferries don't run the same way anymore, not like when I was a boy. I remember the smell of salt and coal smoke, the cries of the gulls overhead as we passed that lady, green as hope herself. Everyone was eager to get to the other side, ready to start fresh.

I remember the swelter of that summer well, the kind that sticks to you like glue. We were shipping out soon, and the world felt like it was holding its breath, waiting to see what would happen next. Even the fireworks seemed to echo the uncertainty in our hearts.

The desert sun beat down, relentless, as we marched. I remember thinking it was a strange way to celebrate, so far from home and everything we were supposedly fighting for. The sand tasted like grit and fear that day.

Seems like yesterday we were still getting used to those new M1 rifles. Hot as blazes, even up in Scotland, but we knew what we were training for, that the whole world was counting on us. We were heading into the thick of it, and every one of us felt it in our bones.
The waves were so high, they crashed over the bow of the ship. Every man on deck was soaked to the bone, praying we'd make it through the night. I never felt so small, or so far from home.

The salt spray stung my face as I clung to the railing, each wave a cold fist punching the bow. Nights were the worst, the endless black swallowing everything but the churning water and the groaning of the ship. I still feel that icy dread sometimes, even now, so many years later.

I remember the endless grey, the cold biting through my pea coat, the constant roll of the deck under my boots. We were just kids, really, staring into that big empty space, hoping to spot a U-boat before it spotted us. Every sunrise felt like a gift back then.

The desert was a furnace, and we were just trying to stay alive. I remember writing letters home, hoping they'd reach my sweetheart before anything happened. Funny, the things you miss most when you're so far away, like a cool glass of lemonade on a summer's day.

The sun was relentless there, beating down on us as we dug trenches in the sand. Nights brought a cold that bit right through the thin blankets they gave us, and I remember just shivering, dreaming of home. I still can't stand the smell of dates; we ate so many, I thought I'd never taste anything else again.

I remember the desert wind, how it whipped sand into every crease of my uniform. We pushed hard through that North African country, chasing after Rommel's ghost. The date palms offered little shade, but the smell of mint tea brewing always gave us hope.

I spent my days out on the flight line, grease under my fingernails and the roar of those Pratt & Whitneys in my ears. Making sure every bolt was tight, every wire was right, before those boys took to the sky – that was my responsibility. Didn't get much glory, but knowing I helped bring them home safe, that was enough.

The desert wind always carried the scent of JP-4, even years later I can still smell it. I spent so many nights under the moonlight, listening to the engines whine as we prepped them for the next day's mission. There was a real camaraderie in that line shack; we were all just trying to keep those birds in the air and the boys safe.

The desert sun was brutal on those aluminum birds, baking them from dawn till dusk. I remember sweating through my fatigues every day, making sure she was ready to fly. Felt a real sense of pride keeping her in the air, knowing those boys depended on us back home.

The sun-baked hills always reminded me of a painting, each town a splash of color on the canvas. We were there longer than I expected, but somehow, the food and the wine made it bearable. Even now, the smell of oregano and diesel takes me right back.

The smell of espresso still takes me back. I can almost feel the sun on my face, hear the vespas buzzing through those narrow streets, and taste the sweet gelato melting in my hand. It was a lifetime ago, but the warmth of the people and the beauty of the countryside, that's something a fella never forgets.

The rolling hills of Tuscany always come to mind when I smell fresh basil and tomatoes. I remember struggling to communicate with the locals, but a shared love of food always bridged the gap. Even now, I sometimes hum those old opera tunes I heard in the piazzas.

The heat there was something fierce, baking the very ground you walked on. We were supposed to be supporting the push north, but mostly we just waited, hoping for a letter from home and praying the next shipment of fuel wouldn't get lost. Funny the things you remember, like the taste of the local wine, strong and rough, but welcome after a long day.

The wind always seemed to carry the scent of olives and the distant sea. I can still feel the grit of the airfield dust between my teeth, even after all these years. Nights were spent huddled together, listening to the crackle of the radio and praying for dawn.

I remember endless days baking under that sun, the wind whipping dust devils across the plains. We were so far from home, fighting for something we barely understood, near that big air base in Italy. Olives and grapes tasted like freedom after weeks of K-rations.

My old P-51 was my pride and joy back then. We flew countless missions together, always watching each other's backs. Quite a few good men and machines didn't make it home, but we kept fighting 'til the job was done.

I still remember the smell of leather and sweat in that old barn, us kids huddled together, dreaming of open ranges and wild adventures. We were a ragtag bunch, bound by a shared spirit and a thirst for something more than just farm life. Every practice felt like we were forging our own destiny, one bucking bronco at a time.
Back in '44, they gave us young fellas wings, alright. Trained us up in P-51s, sleek silver birds they were. Never forget the rumble of those engines, heading up to meet the Luftwaffe.

Seems like just yesterday we were all bright-eyed kids, dreaming of the future. Now, every few years, we gather, a little grayer, a little slower, but still connected by those shared memories of youthful shenanigans and nervous anticipation. It's a comforting reminder that even as time marches on, some bonds remain unbroken.

The gymnasium still smelled faintly of floor wax and teenage angst, even after all these years. Seeing those familiar faces, now lined with time and experience, brought a lump to my throat. We swapped stories, a mix of laughter and quiet reflection, about the dreams we chased and the paths we never expected to wander down.

The old gang gets smaller every time we meet. We swap stories about the kids and grandkids, but you can see the unspoken thoughts in our eyes, the names we don't mention anymore. Funny how the years change a fella, but the memories of '68 still feel like yesterday.

Seems like only yesterday I was tossing my hat in the air with all the boys. Now, most of us are pushing eighty, if we're lucky enough to still be around. Time sure does fly when you're looking back on it.

I can almost smell the sweat and cheap beer from that sweltering June day. Feels like just yesterday we were throwing our hats in the air, young and full of piss and vinegar, ready to take on the world. Now, looking back, it's more like the world took a good chunk out of us.

I can still remember the taste of victory that day, sweeter than any soda pop. Eisenhower was still in office, and we all felt like we were on the cusp of something big, ready to take on the world. Now, looking back, it feels like a lifetime ago, a sepia-toned photograph of youthful dreams.

I remember those entertainers coming overseas, always with a smile and a song, trying to boost our spirits. They brought a little bit of home with them, a reminder of what we were fighting for back there. Even now, I can almost hear the laughter and applause echoing in those makeshift theaters.

The coffee was always weak, but the smiles were strong, and the music, oh, the music could lift you right out of the dust and heat. I remember the scent of perfume, a fleeting reminder of home, hanging in the air amidst all that khaki. Lord, we needed that.

I remember those days overseas like they were yesterday. Stars like Bob Hope would come visit our camp, bringing a little bit of home with them. It meant the world to us young guys, stuck so far away from everything we knew.

I remember being stationed in the Pacific, feeling homesick as could be. Then her voice would come on the radio, clear as a bell, singing those tunes of home. It lifted all our spirits, a little piece of America right there in the jungle.

I remember her voice like it was yesterday, clear as a bell over the static of the radio. Up there in the Pacific, so far from home, that sound was the closest thing to comfort a fella could find. It made the whole damn war feel a little less lonely.

I remember tuning into the Bob Hope show every week, no matter where we were stationed. Her voice, clear as a bell, always reminded me of home, even when the bombs were falling. She was a real morale booster, that's for sure.

The sun was always so bright there, reflecting off the water. We landed near the docks, and the smell of lemons and diesel hung heavy in the air. It was supposed to be a short stay, but things rarely go as planned in wartime.

The air was thick with the smell of lemons and diesel, a strange mix that always takes me back. I remember the sun beating down on the cobblestones, so hot you could fry an egg on them, and the sound of the sea always nearby. Even now, I can almost taste the granita I used to get from that little stand by the harbor.

The opera house was grand, even then, though the whispers of "Cosa Nostra" followed us everywhere, a shadow in the sun. I remember Mama warning me about the markets, said they had eyes and ears, even if the arancini were the best I ever tasted. It was beautiful, sure, but you felt it in your bones – this island held secrets older than time.

The sun was brutal, beating down on us as we trudged inland. Dust devils danced in the distance, mocking our thirst. Found some lemons growing wild, though - never tasted anything so good after weeks at sea.

The sun beat down on us relentlessly that summer, and the smell of lemons was everywhere, almost masking the harsh scent of gunpowder. Every night, I prayed to see another sunrise over that rocky coast, hoping it wouldn't be my last. I still have nightmares about those beaches.

The sun was hot, the ground was dusty, and the air smelled of lemons and sea salt. We landed there, young and scared, tasked with a job that changed us forever. It was supposed to be a quick push, but the fighting was fierce, house to house, town to town. I still remember the taste of the local wine, bitter at first, but welcome after a long day.

The desert sun was brutal, baking the sand and metal alike. We were ordered to secure the area, a vital supply point, though the air hung heavy with the constant threat of a stray spark. One wrong move and we'd all be swallowed by a roaring inferno.

The air always hung thick and heavy there, a greasy sweetness that coated the back of your throat. Even now, I can almost feel the sun reflecting off the shimmering puddles, a mirage promising relief that never came. We were always so careful, one stray spark and it all could have gone up in flames.

The desert sun beat down mercilessly as we guarded that precious liquid gold. Every drop meant life or death for our advance, a lifeline stretching back to the coast. We knew the risks, one stray bullet and it all could go up in flames.

Back then, numbers were my friends, not just figures on a paycheck. I loved how they fit together, the way a problem could be cracked with the right equation. Spent hours lost in that world, felt like I was building something real and true.

Old Joe here never understood why they needed me to calculate the trajectory. Seemed like a waste of time, all those numbers swimming in my head, when all I wanted was to be back home, smelling Momma's apple pie. Funny, though, even now I sometimes see those equations in my dreams, like ghosts of a war I never truly fought.

Back then, folks didn't see much use for anything beyond balancing the books. I remember my grandpa saying, "Figures are figures, that's all you need." Never understood why I found such beauty in those abstract equations, but something about them just felt like uncovering a universal truth.

I remember when they put up that big structure downtown, right after the war. It seemed so tall back then, the way it towered over everything else on Main Street. You could see the whole town from the top floor if you knew the right people.

I remember the echoing quiet inside, the way the light slanted through the tall windows onto the worn wooden floors. You could smell dust and old paper, and sometimes, faintly, the lingering scent of pipe tobacco. For a kid, it felt like a place where secrets gathered in the corners.

I remember when the whole family squeezed into that place. Grandma on the first floor, us kids up top with the creaky stairs, and Ma and Pa in the middle, always running between. It wasn't much, but during the Depression, having a roof over your head like that felt like winning the lottery.

She always had a kind smile, even when I was acting up in the back row. I never understood her passion for history until I lived through some myself. Now, looking back, I wish I'd paid more attention to her stories of the past.

She had a way of making even fractions seem like a grand adventure, her voice a warm hum that filled the classroom on cold winter mornings. You could smell the chalk dust and hear the rustle of paper as we worked, but it was her patience I remember most. I still have the little note she wrote me, telling me to never stop asking questions.

Back then, they said she was too progressive, teaching us about folks like Du Bois and Carver when the other classes were still on the Civil War. A few parents raised a fuss, but she stood her ground, always encouraging us to think for ourselves. Looking back, I realize she was preparing us for a world that wasn't quite ready for us yet.

text: The pawn shop owner saw me coming a mile away, I bet. Still, I polished that brass until it gleamed like gold, picturing myself back in the USO, serenading the troops. Never did make it big, but those melodies kept me company through thick and thin, a faithful friend in a lonely world. Now, the grandkids want to know what all the fuss is about, so maybe it's time to teach them a tune or two.

The brass felt heavy in my hands, each key worn smooth from years of practice. I remember the smoky haze of the jazz clubs, the way the music vibrated through my whole being. Those late nights chasing the perfect note... they're a lifetime away now.

Back then, every kid on my block dreamed of getting one. I remember saving every penny from my paper route, just picturing myself up on stage in some smoky club, the brass shining under the lights. Never did make it big, but those late-night practice sessions echoing through the neighborhood are memories I still cherish.

I remember trading my civilian clothes for fatigues. The letters I wrote home always started with "somewhere in France," but my folks knew better. We were all in it together, patching them up and praying for a swift end to the fighting.

The desert heat was brutal, but seeing those young boys, barely men, looking up at us with such hope... that's what kept us going. We were angels in green, a promise whispered in the dust, a touch of home amidst the chaos. The smell of antiseptic and sweat is forever burned in my memory.

My uniform wasn't exactly camouflage, but it blended in with the tents and the weary faces. We were all just trying to patch people up and send them home, or at least give them a little comfort. Being near the front lines changed you, made you appreciate a warm cup of coffee and a kind word more than anything.

They sent me back stateside after I caught that shrapnel in the Ardennes. Woke up in a place with long corridors and the smell of antiseptic, somewhere near Cleveland I think. Nurses were kind, but all I wanted was to get back home to Ohio.

The smells of antiseptic and old linoleum still haunt me, even after all these years. I remember the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead, casting a sterile glow on everything. It was a lonely place to be, waiting for news, hoping for a miracle.

I remember being shipped back stateside after the Bulge, feeling more like a ghost than a soldier. They put me in a place up in Cleveland, a converted school if I recall right. A lot of boys came home broken, inside and out, and that's where they tried to put us back together again.

I remember when the Wright brothers came back home after Kitty Hawk. The whole town turned out, cheering and waving flags as they rode down Main Street. Seems like everyone there had some connection to the aircraft industry back then.

The smell of the Miami River always brings me back. Summers were sweltering, but we'd cool off by the fountains downtown, all the kids splashing and laughing. Funny how a scent can unlock a whole lifetime.

We'd drive in from the farm on Saturdays, the whole family piled into the Ford. I remember staring up at the airplane displays at the park downtown, dreaming of flight as a young boy. That town was the heart of innovation back then, everyone knew it.

Every Sunday, rain or shine, we'd gather. The stained glass always cast such colorful patterns on the pews, especially during the morning service. It was more than just a building; it was the heart of our community, a place of solace and shared faith.

The old wooden pews always smelled of lemon polish and hymn books. Sunlight streamed through the stained glass, painting the dust motes dancing in the air like tiny angels. It was a place of quiet reflection, where worries seemed to fade with the organ's gentle hum.

Back then, every Sunday morning, you'd see families dressed in their finest heading up the hill. The bell would ring, calling everyone to gather and share hymns, prayers, and maybe a bit of gossip after. It was the heart of our community, where we celebrated everything from baptisms to farewells.

I remember the fear in my mother's eyes when I started turning that strange color. Doc Baker came by every day, shaking his head and muttering about my liver. It was a long, sickly summer, and I mostly remember lying in bed, too weak to even play outside with the other kids.

The fever broke, but I was left feeling weak and looking in the mirror, well, let's just say I wasn't feeling like my usual self. Everything tasted bitter, even the thought of my favorite apple pie. It took weeks before I had my old color back.

The medic said it was going around, especially bad in the camps. Whole companies were laid up, skin and eyes turning the color of a lemon. We just hoped it wouldn't get to us before we shipped out to the Pacific.
